paper_title: Field Mapping in the Tatacoa Desert

methods.description: Traditional geologic mapping was performed to decipher superposition relations amongst beds and bed sets in the Tatacoa Desert using physical tools and techniques.

steps.description: Traditional geologic mapping was performed at 1:25.000 scale using Brunton compass, colored pencils, and paper maps.
steps.searchable_summary: perform geologic mapping using traditional tools

steps.description: Field mapping focused on deciphering superposition relations by following prominent cliff-forming strata along-strike for several kilometers.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze superposition relations by following cliff-forming strata

steps.description: Systematic collection of control points using hand-held GPS units in the northern third of the mapping area.

methods.description: Digitization of field maps and post-processing of geologic contacts using QGIS and Google Earth imagery.

steps.description: Once the field map was completed, the contacts were digitized in QGIS and their location accuracy was checked using Google Earth satellite imagery.
steps.searchable_summary: digitize field maps and verify with satellite imagery

steps.description: All contacts, control points, and dip data are contained in .kml files.
steps.searchable_summary: store contacts and data in KML files

methods.description: Generation of a detailed 3D model using vertical photographs and analysis of bed attitudes using OpenPlot.

steps.description: Collected 338 vertical photographs at 60 m-altitude to generate a detailed 3D model of 7.83 Ha using Agisoft Metashape software.
Converted a 131-million-point cloud into a 2.8-million faces mesh with photographic texture.
steps.searchable_summary: convert point cloud to mesh with texture

steps.description: Used OpenPlot in 101 triangles joining single bed points to measure attitudes.
